metadata.description: Cancer screening awareness knowledge base for rural India. Covers oral, cervical, lung, and breast cancer. Built from NCI, CDC, ACS, WHO, MoHFW-NPCC, ICMR clinical guidelines.
metadata.disclaimer: This knowledge base is for screening awareness only. It does not diagnose cancer. All escalation actions direct users toward qualified healthcare professionals.

escalation_levels.urgent_refer: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 1–3 days. High clinical concern.
escalation_levels.consult: Visit a PHC within 1–2 weeks. Moderate concern warranting professional evaluation.
escalation_levels.monitor: Recheck symptoms in 2–4 weeks. Low concern but stay observant.

scoring_guide.description: Each rule carries a weight (0.0–1.0). The risk engine sums weights of all matched rules for a cancer type to produce a raw score, then normalises to 0–100.
scoring_guide.red_flag_override: If any matched rule has red_flag=true, escalation_level is forced to urgent_refer regardless of score.
scoring_guide.symptom_alone_triggers: If true, this symptom alone (without any risk factor present) is sufficient to trigger its escalation_level.
scoring_guide.combination_escalation: If a user reports symptoms from multiple cancer types, each type is scored independently.

change_log.v1.0: Initial release — oral (10 rules) + cervical (10 rules).
change_log.v2.0: Added lung (10 rules) + breast (10 rules). Total: 40 rules. Fixed LC-007 duration_threshold from 'persistent' to '>3 weeks'.

rules.primary_symptom: Any ulcer or sore in the mouth that does not heal
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have a sore in my mouth that has not healed in over 2 weeks.' Do not wait for pain — painless ulcers are the most common early presentation.
rules.plain_language_reason: A sore in your mouth that has not healed after 2 weeks can be an early sign of mouth cancer. Finding it early means it is much easier to treat. Please see a doctor — even if it is not painful.
rules.notes: Lip and oral cavity cancer is the most frequent cancer in Indian males (15.6% of all cancer cases per ICMR). Non-healing ulcers are the highest-probability early presentation. Squamous cell carcinoma is the most common histological type.

rules.primary_symptom: White (leukoplakia) or red (erythroplakia) patch on gums, tongue, or inner cheek lining
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 1 week. Point out the white or red patch inside your mouth to the doctor. Ask for a referral to a dental surgeon or district hospital for further examination.
rules.plain_language_reason: White or red patches inside the mouth can slowly turn into cancer over time. A doctor can check this with a simple, painless examination. It is better to catch this early before any change happens.
rules.notes: Leukoplakia (white patches that do not rub off) and erythroplakia (red velvety patches) are pre-malignant lesions. Erythroplakia carries ~50% risk of malignant transformation. Both are strongly associated with tobacco and betel quid use in India.

rules.primary_symptom: Growth, lump, or swelling in the mouth, jaw, or cheek
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 3 days. Tell the doctor how long the lump has been present and whether it is growing. Bring a family member if possible.
rules.plain_language_reason: A lump or swelling in your mouth or jaw that does not go away after 2 weeks needs to be checked by a doctor. It may not be painful, but that does not mean it is harmless. Early checking is the safest step.
rules.notes: Swelling of the jaw can cause dentures to fit poorly or teeth to shift. Lumps in the floor of the mouth or cheek that persist beyond 2 weeks without a clear dental cause (e.g. abscess) must be referred. HPV-16 is an established cause of oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

rules.primary_symptom: Difficulty in fully opening the mouth (restricted mouth opening / trismus)
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC this week. Tell the doctor you cannot open your mouth fully. Mention your paan or tobacco use. This condition — called oral submucous fibrosis — is treatable but can become cancerous if ignored.
rules.plain_language_reason: Not being able to open your mouth as wide as before — especially if you chew paan or supari — can be a sign of a condition that sometimes turns into cancer. A doctor can check and help manage this early.
rules.notes: Oral submucous fibrosis (OSMF) is a pre-malignant condition caused by areca nut / betel quid use, highly prevalent in South and Southeast Asia. It causes progressive stiffening of the oral mucosa. Malignant transformation rate is 7-13%. This symptom alone — especially with paan/supari use history — is sufficient for urgent referral.

rules.primary_symptom: Unexplained bleeding, persistent pain, or numbness in the mouth or lip
rules.escalation_action: Go to a PHC or Emergency department today. Unexplained bleeding in the mouth that has no clear cause (such as a recent cut or tooth problem) requires same-day medical evaluation. Do not wait.
rules.plain_language_reason: Bleeding in your mouth that has no clear reason — not from a cut or tooth problem — is always a warning sign that needs to be checked the same day. Numbness in the mouth or lip with no known cause is also a red flag.
rules.notes: Unexplained oral bleeding or numbness (anaesthesia/paraesthesia) with no identifiable dental cause is a high-priority red flag. Numbness indicates possible nerve involvement — a sign of more advanced disease. Any duration is sufficient to trigger urgent referral per NPCC protocol.

rules.primary_symptom: Sore throat that does not go away, or feeling that something is stuck in the throat
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 1 week if you use tobacco or alcohol. Visit within 2 weeks if you do not. Tell the doctor the sore throat has not improved with standard remedies and how long it has lasted.
rules.plain_language_reason: A sore throat that lasts more than 3 weeks and does not improve — especially if you use tobacco or alcohol — can sometimes be a sign of cancer at the back of the mouth or throat. A doctor can check this quickly.
rules.notes: Oropharyngeal cancers (back of tongue, soft palate, tonsils) often present as a persistent sore throat or globus sensation (feeling of something stuck). HPV-16 is an increasingly important cause globally and in urban India. This symptom alone without risk factors is a 'consult' trigger; with tobacco/alcohol/HPV risk factors, escalate to 'urgent_refer'.

rules.primary_symptom: Unexplained loose teeth with no dental decay, gum disease, or injury to explain it
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 3 days. Tell the doctor that your teeth have become loose and that there is no known dental reason (no decay, no injury). A Medical Officer must rule out bone involvement.
rules.plain_language_reason: If your teeth are becoming loose and there is no dental problem — no decay, no injury — this can sometimes be a sign that changes are happening in your jaw bone. A doctor needs to check this quickly.
rules.notes: Loosening of teeth caused by jaw bone invasion (mandibular or maxillary) is a sign of locally advanced oral cancer. Dentists will first rule out periodontal disease; if no dental cause is found, immediate oncology referral is required. This symptom is often the first noticed by the patient themselves.

rules.primary_symptom: A painless swollen lump or enlarged lymph node in the neck that does not go away
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 3 days. Tell the doctor about the lump in your neck and how long it has been there. Mention any mouth sores or patches you have also noticed, even if they seem minor.
rules.plain_language_reason: A painless lump in your neck that lasts more than 3 weeks can be a sign that your body is reacting to something in your mouth or throat. Even if you feel no pain, it should be checked by a doctor within a few days.
rules.notes: Cervical lymphadenopathy (painless neck lump >3 weeks) is often the first sign patients seek medical care for, even when the primary oral lesion was present earlier. It may indicate regional spread (Stage III+). However, this is also an early screening opportunity — it does not always mean advanced disease. Critical: A painless lump is more suspicious than a painful one (painful often = infection).

rules.primary_symptom: Unexplained persistent pain in one ear with no ear infection, hearing loss, or discharge
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 2 weeks. Tell the doctor about the ear pain and confirm there is no ear infection. Mention your tobacco or alcohol use if applicable. Ask if your mouth and throat should also be examined.
rules.plain_language_reason: Ear pain that keeps coming back — when there is no ear infection or hearing problem — can sometimes be a sign of a problem in your throat or mouth sending pain signals to your ear. It is worth asking a doctor to also check your mouth and throat.
rules.notes: Referred otalgia (ear pain originating from oropharyngeal structures via CN IX/X pathways) is a recognised indirect symptom of oropharyngeal and tongue base cancers. It is often dismissed as an ear problem. Alone it is a 'consult' trigger; if combined with a neck lump or throat symptom, escalate to 'urgent_refer'. Requires combination of symptoms to reach high-risk threshold.

rules.primary_symptom: New unexplained change in voice quality, hoarseness, or muffled speech
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 1 week if you use tobacco or alcohol. Visit within 2 weeks if you do not. Tell the doctor your voice has changed without any obvious reason (not a cold or infection).
rules.plain_language_reason: If your voice has sounded different for more than 3 weeks — hoarse or muffled — without a cold or obvious reason, this should be checked by a doctor. It can sometimes be a sign of changes in your throat, especially if you use tobacco.
rules.notes: Persistent hoarseness or voice change >3 weeks is a warning sign for laryngeal or hypopharyngeal cancer, which often coexists with or follows oral cavity cancers in high tobacco-use populations. In heavy tobacco users (bidi smoking is a major risk in rural India), escalate to 'urgent_refer'. Hoarseness alone in a non-smoker is 'consult' level.

rules.primary_symptom: Vaginal bleeding or spotting after sexual intercourse (not related to a menstrual period)
associated_risk_factors: no prior VIA or Pap smear screening
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest Primary Health Centre (PHC) within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have bleeding after sex that is not my period.' Ask about a VIA (Visual Inspection with Acetic Acid) test — it is free, quick, and available at most government PHCs. Do not wait even if there is no pain.
rules.plain_language_reason: Bleeding after sex is not normal and is one of the earliest warning signs of changes in the cervix. A doctor can do a quick, painless check. Finding a problem early makes it much easier to treat — please do not wait.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an early-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms). Post-coital bleeding is caused by a fragile, abnormal cervical surface that bleeds on contact. It is the most actionable early symptom — painless and visible. HPV 16 and 18 cause 70% of cervical cancers worldwide (NCI, Aug 2024). In India, cervical cancer is the leading gynaecological cancer killer; VIA is the MoHFW-endorsed frontline screening tool at PHC level.

rules.primary_symptom: Any vaginal bleeding or spotting after menopause (after periods have permanently stopped)
rules.escalation_action: Go to a PHC or district hospital today. Tell the doctor: 'My periods stopped but I have started bleeding again.' Any bleeding after menopause is an absolute warning sign and must be examined the same day. Ask for a VIA test or specialist referral.
rules.plain_language_reason: Any bleeding from the vagina after your periods have stopped for good is always a warning sign that must be checked by a doctor the same day. There is no normal reason for this to happen, and catching the cause early makes it far easier to treat.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an early-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms). Any vaginal bleeding after menopause has no normal explanation and must be investigated immediately. The post-menopausal period is when cervical cancer incidence peaks in unscreened Indian women. Often dismissed as 'old age spotting' in rural communities — this is a critical misunderstanding that delays diagnosis.

rules.primary_symptom: Vaginal bleeding between periods, or periods that are heavier or last much longer than usual (not explained by other known cause)
associated_risk_factors: no prior cervical screening (VIA or Pap)
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I am bleeding between my periods, or my periods have become much heavier or longer than before, without any clear reason.' Ask about a VIA screening test. Do not dismiss this as a hormonal problem without a doctor's examination.
rules.plain_language_reason: Bleeding that happens between your normal periods, or periods that are much heavier or longer than usual, can be an early warning sign of changes in the cervix. This should always be checked by a doctor — do not assume it is just a hormonal change.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an early-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms). Intermenstrual or abnormal uterine bleeding is frequently attributed to hormonal changes or fibroids in rural settings, causing dangerous diagnostic delays. NCI (Aug 2024) confirms that people who become sexually active before age 18 or have multiple sexual partners are more likely to acquire high-risk HPV. This symptom is particularly likely to be dismissed in younger women — it must not be.

rules.primary_symptom: Unusual vaginal discharge that is watery, has a strong or foul odour, or contains blood — not explained by a known infection
associated_risk_factors: HIV infection or weakened immune system
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'I have had an unusual discharge for over 2 weeks — it has a strong smell and/or contains blood.' Mention that the discharge did not go away with any home treatment. Ask whether a VIA test or swab examination is needed.
rules.plain_language_reason: A discharge from the vagina that has a strong or unusual smell, or that contains blood, and has lasted more than 2 weeks, should be checked by a doctor. It can sometimes be a sign of changes in the cervix that need treatment — even if it does not cause much pain.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an early-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms): 'vaginal discharge that is watery and has a strong odor or that contains blood.' This symptom is very commonly misdiagnosed as a vaginal infection (bacterial vaginosis or trichomoniasis) in rural India, leading to repeated antibiotic prescriptions without cervical examination. NCI (Aug 2024) confirms HIV and weakened immunity as established co-factors that allow HPV to persist and progress. A discharge lasting >2 weeks that does not respond to standard infection treatment must trigger cervical evaluation.

rules.primary_symptom: Persistent pain in the lower abdomen or pelvis, or pain during sexual intercourse — not explained by menstruation, injury, or known condition
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'I have had pain in my lower belly or during sex for more than 3 weeks and it has not gone away.' Mention your smoking status if applicable. Ask for a pelvic examination and, if available, a VIA screening test.
rules.plain_language_reason: Pain in your lower belly or during sex that does not go away after 3 weeks — and has no clear cause like a period or injury — should be checked by a doctor. It can sometimes be a sign of changes in the cervix, especially when it happens along with any unusual bleeding.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an early-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms): 'pelvic pain or pain during sex.' NCI (Aug 2024) confirms smoking increases cervical cancer risk, and the risk increases with amount smoked or secondhand smoke exposure. Pelvic pain alone is non-specific (many gynaecological causes), so it is a 'consult' level trigger unless combined with a bleeding symptom (CC-001/002/003/004), which should prompt escalation to 'urgent_refer'. Pain during intercourse (dyspareunia) in the context of an unscreened woman is always worth investigating.

rules.primary_symptom: Difficulty or pain while urinating, or blood in the urine — not explained by a known urinary tract infection
associated_risk_factors: HIV infection or weakened immune system
history of abnormal Pap or VIA result
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have pain when I urinate, or I see blood in my urine, and I do not think it is a regular urine infection.' This must be examined together with a pelvic assessment. Do not accept a UTI diagnosis without a pelvic check if you have other symptoms from this list.
rules.plain_language_reason: Pain when urinating, or seeing blood in your urine, that does not get better with treatment and has no confirmed infection is a warning sign that should be checked quickly. A doctor needs to examine your pelvis, not just your urine, to find the true cause.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an advanced-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms): 'difficult or painful urination or blood in the urine.' This symptom indicates possible bladder wall involvement from cervical tumour extension. It is critically and commonly misdiagnosed as a urinary tract infection (UTI) in rural PHC settings in India — particularly dangerous because repeated UTI treatment delays oncological referral. Red flag trigger if no confirmed bacterial UTI is identified after initial treatment. NCI screening guidelines (Feb 2025) specifically note that more frequent screening is recommended for HIV-positive women.

rules.primary_symptom: Difficulty or pain during bowel movements, or bleeding from the rectum specifically during a bowel movement — not explained by haemorrhoids or known bowel condition
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have pain when I pass stools, or I see blood when I go to the toilet, and it has been happening for over 2 weeks.' Mention any other symptoms from this list. Do not accept a haemorrhoids diagnosis without a full pelvic examination.
rules.plain_language_reason: Pain when passing stools, or bleeding from the back passage during a bowel movement, that has been happening for more than 2 weeks and has no clear cause (like piles) should be checked urgently. If you also have any unusual vaginal bleeding, please see a doctor today.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an advanced-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms): 'difficult or painful bowel movements or bleeding from the rectum when having a bowel movement.' This symptom indicates possible rectal wall involvement from posterior cervical tumour extension. It is frequently and incorrectly attributed to haemorrhoids (piles) in rural India without pelvic examination. The combination of this symptom with any cervical bleeding symptom (CC-001, 002, or 003) is a strong indicator of locally advanced disease requiring urgent referral.

rules.primary_symptom: Persistent dull ache in the lower back that has not been caused by injury, heavy lifting, or a known spinal condition
rules.body_location: lower back / sacral region (referred from pelvic structures)
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 2 weeks. Tell the doctor: 'I have had a dull ache in my lower back for over 3 weeks with no injury or clear physical cause.' If you also have any unusual vaginal bleeding or discharge from this list, mention all of them together — this combination is more significant and should trigger an urgent referral.
rules.plain_language_reason: A dull ache in your lower back that has lasted more than 3 weeks and has no clear physical cause should be checked by a doctor, especially if you also have any unusual vaginal bleeding or discharge. Backache alone can have many causes, but together with other signs it can be more serious.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an advanced-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms): 'dull backache.' This symptom indicates possible involvement of sacral nerve roots or pelvic sidewall structures. NCI (Aug 2024) identifies high parity (many childbirths) and obesity as reproductive risk factors for cervical cancer. Alone, persistent backache is non-specific and is a 'consult' trigger; combined with any bleeding symptom (CC-001/002/003) it becomes 'urgent_refer'. This combination must be flagged by the app's longitudinal tracker.

rules.primary_symptom: Swelling of one or both legs that has appeared without injury, long travel, blood clot history, or other known cause
rules.body_location: legs / lower limbs (lymphatic/venous obstruction from pelvis)
rules.escalation_action: Go to a district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'One or both of my legs have become swollen without any injury, and it has been more than 2 weeks.' Mention any other symptoms from this list — especially any vaginal bleeding. This combination must be examined urgently.
rules.plain_language_reason: If one or both of your legs have swollen up without any injury, long journey, or known health problem, this is a warning sign that something may be pressing on the vessels in your pelvis. Please see a doctor quickly, especially if you have also had any unusual vaginal bleeding.
rules.notes: Directly listed as an advanced-stage cervical cancer symptom by NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms): 'swelling of the legs.' This symptom indicates lymphatic or venous obstruction caused by enlarged pelvic lymph nodes or pelvic tumour pressing on major vessels — a sign of locally advanced or regionally spread disease. In rural India this is often the symptom that finally brings a woman to a health facility, meaning significant diagnostic delay has already occurred. Unilateral leg swelling is more suspicious than bilateral. This symptom alone requires urgent referral regardless of confirmed prior diagnosis.

rules.primary_symptom: Persistent unexplained tiredness (fatigue) combined with unintentional weight loss and/or abdominal pain — with no known illness to explain these
associated_risk_factors: HIV infection or weakened immune system
rules.escalation_action: Visit your nearest PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'I have been feeling very tired and losing weight without trying, and it has been going on for over a month with no known reason.' Mention any other symptoms from this list — especially vaginal bleeding or discharge. The combination of systemic symptoms with any cervical warning sign should trigger an urgent referral.
rules.plain_language_reason: Feeling very tired all the time and losing weight without trying — for more than 4 weeks with no clear reason — should be checked by a doctor, especially if you also have any vaginal bleeding or unusual discharge. These symptoms together need to be taken seriously.
rules.notes: NCI (cancer.gov/types/cervical/symptoms) lists 'feeling tired' and 'pain in the abdomen' as advanced-stage cervical cancer symptoms. Persistent systemic fatigue and unintentional weight loss indicate that the body is under significant metabolic stress, which in the context of other cervical symptoms (CC-001 through CC-009) strongly suggests advanced disease. CDC (Dec 2024) notes HIV as an established risk factor — HIV-positive women have higher rates of HPV persistence and faster cancer progression. This symptom alone at 'consult' level; combined with any bleeding symptom, escalate immediately to 'urgent_refer' via the app's combination logic.

rules.primary_symptom: Persistent cough that does not go away or keeps getting worse over 3 weeks or more
rules.escalation_action: Go to a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have a cough that has not improved in more than 3 weeks.' Ask for a chest X-ray. Do not assume this is only TB — both TB and lung cancer need immediate investigation.
rules.plain_language_reason: A cough that has lasted more than 3 weeks and is not getting better can be an early sign of lung cancer. This is especially important if you smoke or are around smoke at home. A doctor can do a simple chest X-ray to find out what is causing it.
rules.notes: Persistent cough is listed by CDC, ACS, and NCI as the most common early symptom of lung cancer. In India, it is frequently misattributed to TB or chronic bronchitis, causing dangerous diagnostic delays (Lung India, Jan-Feb 2023). A cough lasting >3 weeks with any red-flag feature (haemoptysis, weight loss, hoarseness) should trigger immediate referral. In India, ~27% of lung cancers occur in non-smokers, often women exposed to biomass cooking smoke (Lancet Regional Health — Southeast Asia, 2024). TB and lung cancer can co-exist.

rules.primary_symptom: Coughing up blood or blood-streaked mucus (haemoptysis) — even a small amount
rules.escalation_action: Go to the nearest hospital emergency today. Tell the doctor: 'I am coughing up blood.' Do not wait, even if it is a very small amount. Ask for a chest X-ray and specialist referral immediately.
rules.plain_language_reason: Coughing up blood — even just a few drops — is never normal and must be checked by a doctor the same day. It can be an early warning sign of lung cancer. Do not assume it is from TB or throat irritation without seeing a doctor.
rules.notes: Haemoptysis is cited as the single highest-specificity red-flag symptom for lung cancer (OR 3.2 at 6 months before diagnosis — PMC10124310). It is listed in all major guidelines (NCI, CDC, ACS, NCCN) as requiring immediate investigation. In India, haemoptysis is frequently attributed to TB — however both must be urgently excluded. Any blood in sputum, regardless of quantity, is an absolute red flag for lung cancer and must not be dismissed.

rules.primary_symptom: Chest pain or discomfort that is persistent, and gets worse when breathing deeply, coughing, or laughing
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have chest pain that gets worse when I breathe deeply or cough, and it has not gone away in more than 2 weeks.' Ask for a chest X-ray.
rules.plain_language_reason: Chest pain that does not go away and gets worse when you take a deep breath or cough is not normal and should be checked by a doctor. It can be a warning sign of changes in the lungs that need early attention.
rules.notes: Persistent chest pain, especially pleuritic in nature (worsening on breathing or coughing), is listed by CDC and ACS as a key lung cancer symptom. It can indicate tumour involvement of the chest wall, pleura, or mediastinum. In rural India, chest pain is often attributed to musculoskeletal causes or acidity, causing diagnostic delay. Pancoast tumours (upper lobe) may additionally cause shoulder and arm pain.

rules.primary_symptom: Shortness of breath or new difficulty breathing that is unexplained or getting worse, without a known cause like asthma or heart disease
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I am getting breathless more than before even with small activities, and this has been going on for more than 2 weeks without a known reason.' Ask for a chest X-ray.
rules.plain_language_reason: Getting breathless more than usual — for example, while walking short distances — without a known reason is a warning sign that your lungs may not be working normally. A doctor can do a simple test to find out what is happening.
rules.notes: New or worsening dyspnoea is listed by CDC, ACS and Lung India (2023) as a significant lung cancer symptom. In India, it is very commonly attributed to COPD or post-TB sequelae without further investigation. Wheezing or stridor (noisy breathing) may also accompany dyspnoea when a tumour partially obstructs a bronchus. This symptom combined with cough or weight loss dramatically increases suspicion.

rules.primary_symptom: Hoarseness or unexplained change in voice that has lasted more than 3 weeks
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'My voice has changed or become hoarse and it has not improved in more than 3 weeks.' Ask for a referral to a specialist if there is no clear throat infection causing this.
rules.plain_language_reason: A change in your voice that lasts more than 3 weeks and is not due to a cold or throat infection can sometimes be caused by a growth pressing on the nerves connected to your voice box. A doctor should check this to be safe.
rules.notes: Hoarseness that persists beyond 3 weeks without a clear infectious cause can indicate lung cancer pressing on the recurrent laryngeal nerve (left-sided tumours near the mediastinum are especially likely to cause this). Listed by ACS as a lung cancer symptom. In India this is frequently attributed to prolonged speaking, chewing tobacco, or throat infections — all of which are reversible in days, not weeks. Persistent hoarseness demands investigation.

rules.primary_symptom: Unexplained weight loss of more than 5 kg in 1–2 months without dieting or illness
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have lost a lot of weight without trying or without being sick, in the past 1-2 months.' Ask for a full examination including a chest X-ray.
rules.plain_language_reason: Losing a lot of weight — without trying to diet or being sick with another illness — is a warning sign that something serious may be happening in your body. When combined with a cough, it is especially important to see a doctor right away.
rules.notes: Unexplained weight loss (OR 2.1 in PMC10124310) is one of the seven symptoms significantly associated with lung cancer up to 6 months before diagnosis. In India, weight loss is often attributed to nutritional deficiency or seasonal illness — leading to dangerous diagnostic delays. Weight loss combined with cough is particularly high-risk and should trigger immediate chest X-ray at PHC level. Cachexia is also a sign of advanced cancer.

rules.primary_symptom: Recurring or persistent chest infections, such as pneumonia or bronchitis, that keep coming back or do not fully clear with treatment
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'I keep getting chest infections that do not go away completely, or the same infection has come back more than once.' Ask for a chest X-ray to check if there is something else causing the repeated infections.
rules.plain_language_reason: If you keep getting chest infections like pneumonia or bronchitis that come back again and again or never fully go away, a doctor should check whether something in your lungs is causing them. A chest X-ray can help find out.
rules.notes: Recurring pneumonia or bronchitis in the same lung area can be a sign that a tumour is partially blocking a bronchus, causing secretions to pool and become infected. In India, this is extremely frequently dismissed as repeated TB or seasonal infections. The Lung India (2023) guideline specifically notes that recurrent/unresolved respiratory infections warrant chest CT if CXR is inconclusive. Any pneumonia in a smoker over 40 that does not clear within 4-6 weeks must be further investigated.

rules.primary_symptom: Severe unexplained fatigue and loss of appetite that is persistent and not explained by another known illness
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'I have been very tired for more than 3 weeks and have lost my appetite without any known reason.' Ask for a full physical examination and blood tests.
rules.plain_language_reason: Feeling very tired all the time for more than 3 weeks, and not wanting to eat, can be signs that the body is fighting something serious. If this happens without a clear reason, a doctor should do a check-up to find out why.
rules.notes: Fatigue (OR 1.6 at 6 months pre-diagnosis — PMC10124310) and loss of appetite are listed by CDC and ACS as systemic lung cancer symptoms. In India, these are commonly attributed to anaemia, nutritional deficiency, or seasonal illness, delaying cancer diagnosis. Paraneoplastic syndromes (SIADH, ectopic Cushings) from small-cell lung cancer can cause these constitutional symptoms. When fatigue co-occurs with respiratory symptoms, the threshold for chest X-ray referral should be low.

rules.primary_symptom: Bone pain, especially in the back, hips, or shoulders, that is persistent, worsens at night, and has no history of injury
associated_risk_factors: known lung lesion or previous cancer
rules.escalation_action: Go to a district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have pain in my back or hips that is getting worse, especially at night, and I have not had any injury.' Ask for a chest X-ray and bone investigation.
rules.plain_language_reason: Pain in the bones — like in the back, hips or shoulders — that gets worse at night and was not caused by an injury can sometimes happen when lung cancer has spread. A doctor needs to check this urgently, especially if you also have a cough.
rules.notes: Bone pain (OR 2.7 at 6 months pre-diagnosis — PMC10124310) is a significant pre-diagnostic symptom of lung cancer, typically indicating bone metastasis. The hips, back and shoulders are most commonly affected. In India, bone pain in older adults is nearly universally attributed to arthritis or vitamin D deficiency without imaging. When bone pain is combined with any respiratory symptom (cough, dyspnoea, haemoptysis), lung cancer with metastasis must be urgently excluded.

rules.primary_symptom: Swelling of the face, neck, or arms — especially in the morning — without a known cause such as kidney disease or heart failure
rules.escalation_action: Go to the nearest hospital emergency today. Tell the doctor: 'My face and neck are swollen and it appeared without a known cause like a heart or kidney problem.' This can be a dangerous emergency. Ask for urgent imaging.
rules.plain_language_reason: Swelling in the face, neck, or arms that has appeared for no clear reason — especially in the morning — can be caused by a growth in the chest blocking a major blood vessel. This is an emergency. Please go to a hospital today.
rules.notes: Swelling of the face and arms indicates Superior Vena Cava (SVC) syndrome — a serious complication where a lung tumour (especially right-sided or mediastinal) compresses the SVC vein, blocking blood return from the head and arms to the heart. ACS specifically flags this as a lung cancer emergency that can become life-threatening. It may also cause headache, dizziness, and bluish-red skin discolouration of the chest. This is an absolute red flag requiring same-day emergency care.

rules.primary_symptom: New lump, thickening, or hard mass in the breast or underarm (armpit) that was not there before
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have found a new lump in my breast or armpit.' Ask for a clinical breast examination. Do not wait to see if it goes away on its own.
rules.plain_language_reason: Finding a new lump or hardness in the breast or armpit that was not there before is one of the most important early signs of breast cancer. Most lumps are not cancer, but every new lump must be checked by a doctor as soon as possible — do not wait.
rules.notes: A new breast lump is the most common and best-known early symptom of breast cancer (ACS, NCI, CDC). Hard, irregular, painless lumps have higher cancer risk, but lumps that are soft, round or tender can also be malignant. In India, breast cancer is the leading cancer among women in urban registries (>30% of all female cancers — ICMR-PBCR) and the second most common in rural areas. Women in their 40s are disproportionately affected in India compared to Western countries (ICMR-NCDIR, 2025). Lumps dismissed as cysts or fibroadenomas without examination are a major cause of late diagnosis.

rules.primary_symptom: Change in the size or shape of one breast — one breast looks noticeably different, swollen, or pulled in
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'One of my breasts has changed its size or shape recently.' Ask for a clinical breast examination and referral for imaging if needed.
rules.plain_language_reason: If one of your breasts looks or feels bigger, smaller, or different in shape from the other one — and this change is new — a doctor should examine it. This can sometimes be a sign of breast cancer and should not be ignored.
rules.notes: Any unexplained asymmetry in breast size or shape is listed by ACS, CDC, and NCI as a breast cancer warning sign. It may reflect a growing tumour distorting breast tissue or shrinkage due to fibrosis. In India, body-image stigma and modest clothing often prevent women from noticing or reporting these changes until they are advanced. The MoHFW NP-NCD programme recommends clinical breast examination for all women aged 30-69 at PHC level, but uptake remains very low (<1.6% — ICMR-NCDIR, 2024).

rules.primary_symptom: Skin changes on the breast: puckering, dimpling, redness, or skin that looks like orange peel (peau d'orange)
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'My breast skin has changed — it looks dimpled or red, or it looks like orange peel.' Ask for an urgent examination. Do not assume this is a skin infection.
rules.plain_language_reason: If the skin of your breast looks dimpled, puckered, red, or like the skin of an orange — especially if you are not breastfeeding — this is a warning sign that must be checked by a doctor the same day. It can be a sign of a type of breast cancer that grows quickly.
rules.notes: Dimpling or puckering of breast skin is listed by CDC, ACS, and NCI as a red-flag breast cancer symptom. Peau d'orange (skin resembling orange peel, due to lymphatic blockage) is a hallmark of inflammatory breast cancer (IBC) — a rare but aggressive subtype comprising <5% of breast cancers. In India, IBC may be mistaken for a breast skin infection (mastitis), delaying diagnosis. Red, swollen, dimpled breast skin in a non-lactating woman must be urgently evaluated for IBC. Skin dimpling can also result from invasive lobular carcinoma tethering the Coopers ligaments.

rules.primary_symptom: Nipple that has turned inward (inverted) recently, or any change in the position or shape of the nipple
associated_risk_factors: previous breast biopsy showing abnormal cells
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'My nipple has recently pulled inward or its shape has changed.' Make sure the doctor checks if this is a new change — naturally inverted nipples from birth are not a concern, but a new inversion must be examined.
rules.plain_language_reason: If your nipple has recently turned inward or changed its shape, and this is new for you, a doctor should examine it. A growth behind the nipple can sometimes pull it inward. If your nipple has always been this way since birth, that is usually normal.
rules.notes: Newly inverted nipple is listed by CDC, ACS, NCI, and Breast Cancer Now (Oct 2024) as a key breast cancer warning sign. It occurs when a tumour behind or near the nipple pulls the nipple inward via tethering. Up to 20% of women naturally have flat or inverted nipples from birth — this is not a concern. Only a NEW inversion or change in nipple position is significant. In India, women are often unaware of this symptom and may not examine their nipples regularly.

rules.primary_symptom: Nipple discharge other than breast milk — especially if it is bloody, clear, or from only one breast and one duct
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'Fluid is coming out of my nipple without me breastfeeding — especially if it is bloody or only from one side.' Ask for a breast examination and imaging referral.
rules.plain_language_reason: If fluid is coming out of your nipple without breastfeeding — especially if it is bloody, from only one breast, or keeps happening on its own — it must be checked by a doctor right away. It can be an early sign of breast cancer.
rules.notes: Nipple discharge can be an early sign of breast cancer (PMC3506094). The risk of malignancy is highest when discharge is: bloody, unilateral (one breast only), from a single duct, persistent, and spontaneous (without squeezing). In non-lactating women, bloody nipple discharge carries 7-15% risk of malignancy (PMC3506094). It is associated with DCIS and intraductal papillary carcinoma. About 33% of papillary carcinomas occur beneath the nipple and cause bloody discharge. In India, nipple discharge is commonly attributed to hormonal changes or infection, delaying diagnosis significantly.

rules.primary_symptom: Red, scaly, itchy, or crusty skin on or around the nipple and areola that does not heal with simple skin treatment
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'The skin around my nipple is red, scaly, or crusty for more than 3 weeks, and it has not improved with skin cream.' Do not assume this is eczema — ask for a breast examination.
rules.plain_language_reason: If the skin of your nipple or the area around it is red, flaky, itchy, or crusty for more than 3 weeks and does not get better with skin cream, it could be an early sign of a type of breast cancer that affects the nipple. A doctor should examine it — do not treat it only as a skin problem.
rules.notes: Pagets disease of the breast — a rare form of breast cancer affecting the nipple and areola — causes redness, itching, flaky or thickened skin on the nipple, often mistaken for eczema or dermatitis (NCI, 2024). About 1-3% of all breast cancers are Pagets disease. Symptoms may come and go, increasing the likelihood of misdiagnosis. A flattened nipple and yellow or bloody discharge may accompany this. In India, women with this presentation are frequently treated by dermatologists for skin conditions rather than being referred for breast cancer evaluation.

rules.primary_symptom: Swelling or lump in the armpit (underarm lymph nodes) without a recent injury or infection
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC or district hospital within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have a lump or swelling in my armpit that has not gone away in more than 2 weeks without any injury or infection.' Ask for a full breast and lymph node examination.
rules.plain_language_reason: A lump or swelling in the armpit that does not go away after 2 weeks — without a recent cut, infection, or injury — can mean that breast cancer has spread to the lymph nodes. A doctor must examine this without delay.
rules.notes: Axillary (underarm) lymph node swelling is listed by CDC, ACS, and NCI as a key breast cancer symptom. Breast cancer frequently spreads first to axillary lymph nodes, and swollen nodes can precede or accompany a breast lump. In India, swollen lymph nodes under the arm are very commonly attributed to TB (a very reasonable suspicion in the Indian context), local skin infections, or trauma. Both TB and breast cancer must be excluded urgently. Swollen nodes in a woman over 30 without infection or injury must include breast cancer in the differential.

rules.primary_symptom: Persistent breast pain or tenderness in one area that does not change with the menstrual cycle
rules.escalation_action: Visit a PHC within 1 week. Tell the doctor: 'I have pain in one part of my breast that has been there for more than 3 weeks and does not change with my period.' Ask for a clinical breast examination.
rules.plain_language_reason: Pain in one part of the breast that has lasted more than 3 weeks and does not change with your periods is different from the normal tenderness many women feel. A doctor should check if there is a physical cause, even if breast cancer rarely causes pain in its early stages.
rules.notes: NCI notes that breast cancer does not usually cause pain, and most breast pain is due to benign conditions. However, CDC lists pain in any area of the breast as a symptom that should be reported to a doctor. Persistent, localised (one spot) breast pain lasting more than 3 weeks that does not vary with the menstrual cycle is more clinically significant than cyclical mastalgia (common, benign). In India, breast pain is almost never proactively reported by women to PHC workers due to stigma and privacy concerns — clinical breast examination training for ASHAs and ANMs is critical.

rules.primary_symptom: Redness, warmth, or swelling of the entire breast that appears suddenly and is not related to breastfeeding or a known infection
rules.escalation_action: Go to a district hospital or emergency today. Tell the doctor: 'My entire breast has become red, warm, and swollen quickly and I am not breastfeeding.' Ask for an urgent examination for inflammatory breast cancer — do not assume this is an infection unless confirmed by a doctor.
rules.plain_language_reason: If your whole breast suddenly becomes red, swollen, and warm — and you are not breastfeeding — this is an emergency. There is a type of breast cancer that looks exactly like an infection and grows very fast. A doctor must examine it the same day, not just treat it with antibiotics.
rules.notes: Inflammatory breast cancer (IBC) is a rare but aggressive subtype (<5% of breast cancers) that presents with sudden redness, warmth, swelling, and sometimes peau d'orange of the entire breast — often without a palpable lump. Only 15% of IBC patients have a lump (National Breast Cancer Foundation, 2024). IBC is frequently and fatally misdiagnosed as mastitis or cellulitis and treated with antibiotics in India. If a non-breastfeeding woman has a red, swollen, warm breast that does not improve within days of antibiotics, IBC must be urgently excluded.

rules.primary_symptom: Unexplained weight loss, persistent back pain, or breathlessness in a woman with a known or previous breast lump — possible sign of spread
rules.escalation_action: Go to a district hospital or cancer centre within 3 days. Tell the doctor: 'I have had a breast lump before (treated or untreated) and now I have developed back pain / breathlessness / weight loss / yellow eyes.' Ask for an urgent full-body examination and imaging.
rules.plain_language_reason: If you have previously had a breast lump (even if it was treated) and now you have back pain, trouble breathing, yellow skin, or sudden weight loss — these can be signs that the cancer may have spread. Please see a doctor at a hospital as soon as possible.
rules.notes: Metastatic (Stage 4) breast cancer may present with back or bone pain (bones are the most common metastatic site), shortness of breath (lungs), abdominal swelling or jaundice (liver), or headaches/confusion (brain) — per NCI and ACS. In India, a significant proportion of women present with metastatic disease at first diagnosis because of delayed care-seeking. Women who have had a prior breast lump — especially untreated — and develop any of these systemic symptoms must be urgently investigated for recurrence or spread.
